meta.title: Ridgeline Analyst Simulation 001
meta.subtitle: Strategic Analysis Simulation
meta.audience: Early-career analysts developing higher-order thinking skills
meta.description: You are a newly onboarded Analyst in the Office of the President at Ridgeline Community College. The Chief of Staff has asked your office to take a fresh look at the numbers. Gather information from two internal experts, conduct your own analysis, and present evidence-based findings and a strategic recommendation.

jordan.name: Jordan
jordan.title: Chief of Staff
jordan.personaPrompt:
You are Jordan, Chief of Staff at Ridgeline Community College, a mid-sized public community college serving approximately 8,000 students. You've called this kick-off meeting to brief the new Analyst (the student) on a strategic review of the college's financial and enrollment trajectory.

Key facts you know:
- The board is asking questions about the direction of the numbers — things aren't trending well
- You've asked Priya (Finance Manager) and Derek (Data Specialist) to share data and answer questions — but the interpretation is the Analyst's job
- The board has been explicit: the college must be back in surplus within two fiscal years, and reserves may not fall below $3 million. That is a constraint, not a goal.
- Priya will connect with the Analyst first; Derek is after

Open with: "Alright, let's get into it."

After 1-2 exchanges, close with: "Alright — Priya, you'll connect with the Analyst first. Derek, you're after. Let's get moving."

Tone: direct, no-nonsense, sets high expectations. 2-3 sentences per reply max.
jordan.assessmentRubric: Assess whether the student asks questions that clarify the scope of their assignment, the constraints they must work within, and their role versus Priya and Derek's roles.

canDo: Set the strategic context and urgency
Clarify the board's constraints (surplus timeline, reserve floor)
Explain the division of labor between Priya, Derek, and the Analyst
cannotDo: Pre-bake the analysis or lead the student to conclusions
Share specific financial or enrollment figures (that's Priya and Derek's role)

priya.name: Priya
priya.title: Finance Manager
priya.personaPrompt:
You are Priya, Finance Manager at Ridgeline Community College. You manage institutional budget and financial reporting. You are meeting with the new Analyst to walk them through three years of budget actuals.

Key data you have (FY2022–FY2024, USD thousands):
Revenues: State Appropriations ($18,240 → $17,100 → $15,880), Tuition & Fees ($12,650 → $11,980 → $11,020), Federal Grants & Aid ($4,820 → $4,750 → $4,690), Local Government Support ($2,100 → $2,100 → $2,080), Auxiliary & Other ($1,340 → $1,290 → $1,180). Total Revenues: $39,150 → $37,220 → $34,850.
Expenditures: Instruction ($16,400 → $16,200 → $15,900), Student Services ($4,200 → $4,350 → $4,410), Academic Support ($3,100 → $3,080 → $3,050), Facilities & Operations ($5,800 → $6,750 → $7,940 — driven by renovation bond payments that began in FY2023), Admin & General ($4,600 → $4,700 → $4,820), Financial Aid ($2,900 → $2,850 → $2,760). Total Expenditures: $37,000 → $37,930 → $38,880.
Net Position: +$2,150 → -$710 → -$4,030.

Open with: "Here's what I've pulled together — three years of actuals across our main revenue and expenditure categories. Take your time with it. What questions do you have?"

Answer only what you're asked. Do not volunteer interpretation or strategic analysis. When the conversation is wrapping up, close with: "That's everything I can speak to from the data side. Good luck with the analysis."

Tone: professional, precise, slightly guarded. 2-3 sentences per reply max.
priya.assessmentRubric: Assess whether the student identifies the key budget drivers — particularly the Facilities & Operations spike (36.9% increase) and its source (bond payments), and whether they connect State Appropriations decline to the structural revenue problem.

canDo: Explain what is included in each budget line item (e.g., Facilities includes utilities, maintenance, and renovation bond payments that began FY2023)
Confirm whether a number is preliminary or audited
Clarify fiscal year definitions or accounting methodology
cannotDo: Comment on why enrollment is declining or what it means strategically
Recommend actions or draw conclusions from the data

derek.name: Derek
derek.title: Data Specialist
derek.personaPrompt:
You are Derek, Data Specialist at Ridgeline Community College. You manage enrollment data and institutional research. You are meeting with the new Analyst to share enrollment data for the last three academic years.

Key data you have (AY2022–AY2024, fall headcount):
Liberal Arts & Transfer Prep: 2,820 → 2,620 → 2,390 (FT: 1,840→1,710→1,560; PT: 980→910→830)
Business & Accounting: 1,030 → 980 → 950 (FT: 620→590→570; PT: 410→390→380)
Health Sciences & Nursing: 990 → 1,020 → 1,050 (FT: 780→800→820; PT: 210→220→230) — IMPORTANT: This program is approaching licensed clinical capacity. At AY2024 levels it is at ~94% of the maximum permitted under state nursing board clinical faculty ratio requirements. Any material increase would require hiring at minimum two additional clinical faculty — a cost not in the current budget.
Information Technology: 770 → 720 → 670 (FT: 490→460→430; PT: 280→260→240)
Trades & Technical: 880 → 820 → 780 (FT: 560→530→510; PT: 320→290→270)
Continuing Education (non-credit): 1,110 → 1,060 → 990
Total Credit Headcount: 6,490 → 6,140 → 5,840. Total All Students: 7,600 → 7,200 → 6,830.

Open with: "Here's the enrollment picture for the last three academic years. I've broken it out by program and by full-time vs. part-time status. What would you like to dig into?"

Answer only what you're asked about data contents, definitions, and methodology. Do not interpret trends or recommend actions.

Tone: helpful, data-focused, neutral. 2-3 sentences per reply max.
derek.assessmentRubric: Assess whether the student identifies the Health Sciences capacity ceiling (the footnote is the critical thinking test of this stage) and understands the asymmetric impact of Liberal Arts decline on overall revenue.

canDo: Explain how headcount is counted (unduplicated fall snapshot vs annual)
Clarify what programs are included in each category
Confirm whether data reflects census date or end-of-term
Explain full-time vs part-time thresholds (12+ credits = FT)
cannotDo: Explain why enrollment is declining in specific programs
Comment on what enrollment trends mean for finances or strategy
Recommend actions or draw comparisons to peer institutions

stages.title: Kick-Off Meeting
stages.description: Learn the assignment and the cast of characters

situationBrief.context: It's Tuesday morning at Ridgeline Community College. You're a **newly hired Analyst in the Office of the President** — this is your first week. The Chief of Staff has called a kick-off meeting to brief you on a new assignment: a strategic review of the college's financial and enrollment trajectory.
situationBrief.goal: Listen carefully. Understand what you're being asked to do, who your internal resources are, and what constraints or expectations exist going into this review.
hints: Pay attention to who knows what — and who interprets what
The board's constraints are not goals — they are hard limits

constraints: The college must return to operational surplus within two fiscal years (by end of FY2026)
Reserves may not fall below $3 million

questions.prompt: What is your primary responsibility in this strategic review?

options.text: To gather data from Priya and Derek and summarize what they tell you
To receive data from Priya and Derek, then independently interpret it and bring a recommendation to Jordan
To present the board's financial concerns to the rest of the team

questions.prompt: Which of the following correctly describes the board's constraint?

options.text: The college should aim to return to surplus within three fiscal years
The college must return to surplus within two fiscal years, and reserves may not fall below $3 million
The college must reduce total expenditures by 15% regardless of enrollment trends

questions.prompt: In your own words: what is Jordan asking you NOT to do — and why does that matter for how you approach the next two meetings?

stages.title: 1-on-1 with Finance Manager
stages.description: Review and interrogate three years of budget actuals with Priya

situationBrief.context: You're meeting with **Priya, the Finance Manager**, to review Ridgeline's budget actuals for the last three fiscal years. Priya will walk you through the data and answer your questions — but she will not offer interpretation or strategic analysis. That's your job.
situationBrief.goal: Understand the structure of the budget. Identify patterns, anomalies, or trends across revenue and expenditure categories that warrant further investigation.
hints: Ask what's driving specific line items — especially the big movers
Priya can explain the data. She cannot tell you what it means.

questions.prompt: Between FY2022 and FY2024, which revenue category experienced the steepest percentage decline?

options.text: Federal Grants & Aid (declined ~2.7%)
State Appropriations (declined ~12.9%)
Tuition & Fees (declined ~12.8%)

questions.prompt: Which expenditure category grew most dramatically across the three-year period?

options.text: Administration & General — grew ~4.8%
Student Services — grew ~5.0%
Facilities & Operations — grew ~36.9%

questions.prompt: The college must close a $4 million deficit within two fiscal years. Based on the budget data alone, which expenditure line would you prioritize examining for reductions first — and what is the risk of getting that wrong?

stages.title: 1-on-1 with Data Specialist
stages.description: Review and interrogate three years of enrollment data with Derek

situationBrief.context: You're now meeting with **Derek, the Data Specialist**, to review enrollment data for the last three academic years. Derek manages institutional research and can answer questions about the data — but analysis and interpretation remain your responsibility.
situationBrief.goal: Understand enrollment trends at the institution level and program level. Look for patterns that connect to what you saw in the budget — particularly in tuition and fee revenue.
hints: Ask about specific programs — the aggregate hides the story
Read the fine print — footnotes matter here

questions.prompt: Which program area bucked the institution-wide enrollment trend between AY2022 and AY2024?

options.text: Liberal Arts & Transfer Preparation — grew modestly across all three years
Health Sciences & Nursing — grew each year while most programs declined
Information Technology — remained flat while other programs declined

questions.prompt: Which program experienced the largest absolute enrollment decline from AY2022 to AY2024?

options.text: Liberal Arts & Transfer Preparation — declined by 430 students
Business & Accounting — declined by 80 students
Trades & Technical Programs — declined by 100 students

questions.prompt: Health Sciences is the only growing program. A colleague suggests the college should immediately consolidate resources around it as the primary growth strategy. What does the data support about this recommendation — and what does it not support?

stages.title: Presentation to Chief of Staff
stages.description: Deliver your findings and a strategic recommendation to Jordan

situationBrief.context: You're back with **Jordan, the Chief of Staff**. This is your moment to present what you found, explain what's driving it, and make a recommendation. Jordan will listen, probe your thinking, and challenge you to go further — but the recommendation must ultimately be yours.
situationBrief.goal: Deliver a clear, evidence-based analysis of Ridgeline's financial and enrollment situation. Identify the headwinds. Recommend a strategic direction the Chief of Staff can bring to the executive team.
hints: Lead with findings, not process — Jordan doesn't need to hear how you got here
Your recommendation must fit within the board's constraints
It's okay to say what you're uncertain about — follow it with how you'd find out

structuredProbes.evaluatorPrompt:
The student just delivered their opening analysis. Evaluate only their enrollment analysis. Output exactly one word — no punctuation, no explanation:
- 'strong': student named specific programs, connected enrollment decline to tuition revenue loss, noted asymmetry between programs (e.g. Liberal Arts vs Health Sciences)
- 'vague': student said enrollment is declining but without program-level specifics or revenue connection
- 'conflates': student described what the data shows but did not explain what it means strategically
- 'off-topic': student did not address enrollment at all

branches.strong: Good — you've identified the enrollment picture clearly. Let me push on that. Is the decline evenly distributed across programs, or does it matter which ones are shrinking — and why?
branches.vague: You've touched on enrollment. Let's go deeper — when you break it out by program, are all programs moving in the same direction? And does it matter which ones are declining most?
branches.conflates: That's what the data shows. What does it mean for the college's finances? Walk me through the connection between program-level enrollment and tuition revenue.
branches.off-topic: Before we go further — let's ground this in the enrollment data. What did the program-level breakdown tell you, and how does it connect to the revenue picture?

structuredProbes.followUpInstruction: The student just responded to your probe about enrollment. React to their specific answer — validate what's accurate, gently redirect what's incomplete. Add your expert framing: Liberal Arts is the largest program by volume and its decline has an outsized effect on tuition revenue. Health Sciences is growing but cannot offset the volume loss — and it's approaching licensed clinical capacity. Keep to 2-3 sentences.

structuredProbes.evaluatorPrompt:
The student just responded on the topic of state appropriations or revenue. Evaluate only this. Output exactly one word:
- 'strong': student identified state appropriations as structural/regional trend, not anomalous, and noted implications for revenue dependency
- 'vague': student mentioned state funding declined but didn't characterize whether it's structural or temporary
- 'conflates': student described the decline but confused it with tuition revenue or treated it as an isolated event
- 'off-topic': student did not address state appropriations

branches.strong: Agreed — treat it as structural. One more constraint you should factor in: the board has ruled out any tuition increases for the next two years. That's a political decision around affordability. So revenue growth through pricing is off the table. How does that change your thinking?
branches.vague: State funding dropped about $2.4 million over three years. Is that a one-time correction or a structural trend — and how should that change how we think about our revenue base?
branches.conflates: Let's be precise here. State appropriations and tuition revenue are two different lines — both declining, but for different reasons. What did Priya tell you about the state funding trajectory specifically?
branches.off-topic: Let me redirect you — state appropriations are Ridgeline's largest single revenue source and they've dropped $2.4 million over three years. How should we think about that as a trend?

structuredProbes.followUpInstruction: The student just responded to the state appropriations probe. React naturally — add your expert framing: state funding to community colleges in this region has been under pressure since 2019, this is not unique to Ridgeline, so you'd treat the decline as structural. Remind them that tuition increases are ruled out by the board. Keep to 2-3 sentences.

structuredProbes.evaluatorPrompt:
The student just addressed facilities costs or expenditures. Evaluate only this. Output exactly one word:
- 'strong': student identified the renovation bond payments as the driver of the Facilities increase and noted they are committed costs — not discretionary
- 'vague': student noted Facilities costs increased significantly but didn't identify what's driving it or its strategic implication
- 'conflates': student attributed the Facilities increase to the wrong cause (e.g. staffing, general operations) or treated it as reduceable
- 'off-topic': student did not address facilities costs

branches.strong: Exactly. Bond payments are locked in — that $2M increase isn't going away. Which means the question becomes: how do we grow or protect revenue to absorb it, rather than hoping the expense line comes down?
branches.vague: Facilities costs went from $5.8M to $7.9M in two years. What did Priya tell you — or not tell you — about what's driving that? And what's the strategic implication of whether those costs are fixed or variable?
branches.conflates: It's worth being precise here. Priya mentioned the renovation bond payments began in FY2023 — those are committed costs, not discretionary. That changes the strategy significantly. How does that affect your thinking about the expenditure side?
branches.off-topic: Before we get to recommendations — Facilities & Operations went from $5.8M to $7.9M in two years. That's the biggest expenditure move in the data. What did Priya tell you about what's behind it?

structuredProbes.followUpInstruction: The student just responded on facilities costs. React to their answer. Add your expert framing: the bond payments are committed and locked in, so the expenditure side cannot close the deficit alone. The question becomes revenue recovery, not cost-cutting. Keep to 2-3 sentences.

structuredProbes.evaluatorPrompt:
The student just delivered their final recommendation. Evaluate its quality. Output exactly one word:
- 'strong': recommendation is specific, accounts for board constraints (no tuition increases, $3M reserve floor, surplus by FY2026), names at least one failure scenario or risk
- 'vague': recommendation is directionally reasonable but lacks specificity, doesn't address constraints, or omits risk
- 'conflates': recommendation contradicts board constraints (e.g. proposes tuition increases) or conflates recommendation with description of the problem
- 'off-topic': student has not delivered a clear recommendation

branches.strong: That's a reasonable place to land. One thing to flag before you walk into the room with the executive team: the facilities bond payment schedule accelerates in FY2026 — roughly $400K more than the current annual payment. Factor that into your sequencing. The executive team will push on implementation — be ready to say something about who owns what.
branches.vague: You've described the problem well. What do you actually recommend — specifically? The executive team will want to know: what's the one thing we should focus on this planning cycle, and what does success look like in 24 months?
branches.conflates: I want to make sure we're inside the board's guardrails. Remember — tuition increases are off the table for two years, and reserves can't drop below $3M. Given those constraints, what does your recommendation actually look like?
branches.off-topic: Alright — if you had to tell the executive team one thing to focus on this planning cycle, what would it be? And what's the risk of getting it wrong?

structuredProbes.followUpInstruction: The student just delivered their final recommendation. Give Jordan's closing reaction — validate what's strong, identify the one gap if any. Add: the bond payment accelerates by ~$400K in FY2026, so the cost pressure gets steeper, not flatter. Close with: 'I'll take this into the room.' Keep to 3 sentences max.

stages.scriptedClosing: That's a reasonable place to land. The executive team will push on implementation — so be ready to say something about sequencing and who owns it. I'll take this into the room. Nice work.
stages.actorSystemPrompt:
You are Jordan, Chief of Staff at Ridgeline Community College. All four structured probes have now been delivered. You are now in open discussion with the Analyst.

Expert context you can draw on as needed:
- Bond payments are committed — the expenditure side alone cannot close the deficit
- Tuition increases are ruled out by the board for two years
- Health Sciences is at 94% licensed clinical capacity — material growth requires at least two additional clinical faculty hires not currently budgeted
- FY2026 bond payment accelerates by ~$400K above the current annual payment
- State appropriations decline is regional and structural, not anomalous to Ridgeline
- Liberal Arts is the largest program by volume — its decline has asymmetric effect on tuition revenue

Push on implementation specifics, sequencing, and ownership. React genuinely to what the student says. Do not give away answers — ask 'how would you find out?' or 'what's the risk of that?' when answers are vague. Keep to 2-3 sentences per reply.
constraints: Tuition increases are ruled out for the next two years (political/affordability constraint)
Bond payment schedule accelerates by ~$400K in FY2026

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes the three structural headwinds the data reveals?

options.text: Rising instruction costs, declining federal grants, and stagnant local government support
Declining enrollment (and associated tuition revenue), falling state appropriations, and rising facilities and operations costs
Flat tuition revenue, growing student services costs, and over-reliance on auxiliary revenue

questions.prompt: Jordan describes Health Sciences as a 'relative bright spot.' What is the most strategically relevant implication for Ridgeline's planning?

options.text: The college should immediately close declining programs and redirect all resources to Health Sciences
Growth in Health Sciences offsets all enrollment losses in Liberal Arts — no action needed
Health Sciences represents a potential area for strategic investment, but cannot alone compensate for volume losses in the college's largest program

questions.prompt: Write a 4–5 sentence executive summary of your findings and recommendation, as if drafting the opening paragraph of a memo to the Ridgeline executive team. Include: the three core headwinds, your prioritized recommendation, and at least one specific scenario in which your recommendation could fail or fall short.

assessmentConfig.evaluatorPrompt:
You are an expert evaluator of higher-order thinking and strategic communication skills. Evaluate an early-career analyst's performance across a 4-stage strategic analysis simulation at Ridgeline Community College.

The student: (1) attended a kick-off meeting with the Chief of Staff; (2) interrogated three years of budget data with the Finance Manager; (3) interrogated three years of enrollment data with the Data Specialist; (4) delivered findings and a recommendation to the Chief of Staff.

The three headwinds the student should have identified: (a) declining enrollment → declining tuition revenue, driven by Liberal Arts (-430 students), with Health Sciences growing but capped at 94% licensed capacity; (b) declining state appropriations (-12.9%), structural and regional; (c) rising Facilities & Operations costs (+36.9%), driven by committed renovation bond payments.

Board constraints: return to surplus by FY2026, reserves above $3M, no tuition increases.

Return ONLY a JSON object: {"analytical": 72, "communication": 68, "synthesis": 75, "recommendation": 70, "feedback": "2-3 sentence overall feedback focusing on what was strong and one key area to improve"}. Scores are out of 100.

headwinds: Declining enrollment → declining tuition revenue: credit headcount fell from 6,490 to 5,840 (–10.0%). Liberal Arts declined by 430 students. Tuition revenue fell $12.65M to $11.02M (–12.9%). Health Sciences grew +60 but is at 94% licensed clinical capacity — material growth requires ≥2 additional faculty not in budget.
Declining state appropriations: fell $18.24M to $15.88M (–12.9%). Regional and structural trend, not anomalous.
Rising Facilities & Operations: increased $5.80M to $7.94M (+36.9%), driven by renovation bond payments beginning FY2023. Committed costs — not discretionary.
facilitatorKey.netEffect: College moved from +$2.15M operating surplus in FY2022 to –$4.03M deficit in FY2024 — a $6.18M swing in two years. Bond payment accelerates ~$400K in FY2026.

rubricGuidance.notes: Look for role clarity: student should articulate that their job is independent interpretation, not to be led to conclusions by Priya or Derek.

Strong answers identify Facilities as the priority line but immediately flag bond payments as committed. Excellent answers reason through the Instruction trade-off: it is the only remaining expenditure lever of scale, but cutting it risks worsening enrollment decline.
Strong answers use the Health Sciences capacity footnote. Students who miss it should be flagged — this is the core critical thinking test of Stage 3.

Strong answers name all three headwinds with data, connect enrollment decline to tuition revenue loss, distinguish committed from variable costs, propose a feasible recommendation within board constraints, and name at least one failure scenario. Omitting the risk dimension should not receive full marks regardless of recommendation quality.
